mnemonics.title: Cayley's Formula
mnemonics.content: Cayley's Magic: n^(n-2) trees, it's fine! Think 'n to the (n-2) power gives trees in full flower' - imagine 'n raised to power n-2, Cayley makes trees that are true'.

mnemonics.title: Prüfer Sequence Length
mnemonics.content: For n vertices, sequence length = n - 2. Remember: 'n take 2 away, Prüfer sequence stays' - think of removing 2 endpoints (leaves) from the tree.

mnemonics.title: Tree Properties
mnemonics.content: A tree has: C-onnected, A-cyclic, n-1 edges. Remember: 'Can (C) A (A) Knot (n-1)' - trees CANNOT have knots/cycles!

mnemonics.title: Matrix Tree Theorem
mnemonics.content: Laplacian = Degree - Adjacency. Remember: 'LAD' - Laplacian is A (adjacency) subtracted from D (degree). Take determinant after deleting one row-column.

acronyms.expansion: Cayley's Formula: Number of labeled trees = n^(n-2)
acronyms.usage: Used to count trees when all vertices are distinctly labeled

acronyms.expansion: Prüfer Sequence: Length = n-2, each element from {1,...,n}
acronyms.usage: Encodes tree structure uniquely for labeled trees

acronyms.expansion: L = D - A (Degree matrix minus Adjacency matrix)
acronyms.usage: Used in Matrix Tree Theorem for counting spanning trees

acronyms.expansion: Kirchhoff's Tree Theorem: Spanning trees = any cofactor of Laplacian
acronyms.usage: Count spanning trees in any graph using linear algebra

visualTips.visualization: Imagine n labeled vertices as n different colored balls. The number of ways to connect them in a tree structure equals n raised to power (n-2). Think of building a tree by repeatedly connecting leaves.

Picture a tree where you iteratively remove the smallest leaf and record its neighbor. This creates the sequence. Reverse: start with sequence, add vertices not in sequence as leaves, connect to sequence elements.
Draw the graph, create its Laplacian matrix (diagonal shows degrees, off-diagonal shows -1 if connected, 0 otherwise). Delete any row and column, compute determinant - that's your tree count!
Every tree with 2+ vertices has at least 2 leaves. Visualize a path: endpoints are leaves. Any extra branching creates more leaves at extremities.

analogies.analogy: Like counting different road networks connecting n cities where every city must be reachable and there are no loops - exactly n^(n-2) possibilities!
Think of Prüfer coding like reading a tree's 'DNA'. Just as DNA encodes an organism, this sequence of n-2 numbers uniquely encodes a tree structure.
A spanning tree is like the minimum telephone network connecting n houses - you need n-1 wires to connect all houses without any loops.
Finding spanning trees by matrix methods is like using a mathematical 'sieve' - the Laplacian matrix filters through to give you exactly the count of possible spanning subgraphs.
